findings.title: DoNotDisturb module exported but never consumed by any caller
findings.description: The entire do-not-disturb module (8 functions) is exported from the package index but no consumer in apps/ or any other package imports these functions. The app integration at apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/anima/persona-engine.ts does not use canTrigger, recordTrigger, recordIgnore, or any DND function. This is orphan code with no live entry point. The evaluateTriggers function also does not internally call canTrigger, so the DND system is completely disconnected from the trigger evaluation pipeline.
findings.suggestion: Integrate canTrigger into the evaluate() function in apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/anima/persona-engine.ts. Call canTrigger before evaluateTriggers and pass state.cooldownMultiplier as the cooldownMultiplier parameter. Store DoNotDisturbState alongside lastTriggerTimes and call recordTrigger/recordIgnore to close the loop.

findings.title: 10 of 11 triggers exported but not wired to app entry point
findings.code_snippet:
export { ALL_TRIGGERS, evaluateTriggers, INTIMACY_ORDER,
  meetsIntimacyRequirement, T01_MORNING_GREETING, T02_NOON_CARE,
  T03_REST_REMINDER, T04_ENTERTAINMENT_SWITCH, T05_LATE_NIGHT,
  T06_EVENING_SUMMARY, T07_IMPORTANT_DATE, T08_TASK_DUE,
  T09_HIGH_FREQUENCY_SWITCH, T10_BIG_TASK_COMPLETE,
  T11_RETURN_TO_DESKTOP } from './proactive-trigger'
findings.description: The app integration at apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/anima/persona-engine.ts only imports T03_REST_REMINDER (line 16: `import { ..., T03_REST_REMINDER } from '@proj-airi/persona-engine'`) and passes `const triggers = [T03_REST_REMINDER]` (line 37). The 10 new triggers (T01, T02, T04-T11) and ALL_TRIGGERS are exported but have no consumer reachable from any entry point. These triggers are dead-on-arrival in production.
findings.suggestion: Update apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/anima/persona-engine.ts to import ALL_TRIGGERS instead of just T03_REST_REMINDER, and pass it to evaluateTriggers. This wires all 11 triggers to the live Electron service.

findings.title: lastTriggerTimes and intimacy state held only in closure memory
findings.description: The persona-engine service stores lastTriggerTimes (trigger cooldown tracking), intimacy state, and now implicitly would need DoNotDisturbState in closure-scoped variables. These are lost on Electron process restart. Per CLAUDE.md architecture rules, critical state must be persistable and recoverable. Intimacy score (which affects which triggers can fire) and trigger history (which prevents notification spam) are business state that affects user experience.
findings.suggestion: Persist lastTriggerTimes and intimacy state to electron-store or a local SQLite database. Load on startup, save after each state change. This makes the persona engine restart-safe.

findings.title: DoNotDisturb module not wired into setupPersonaEngine
findings.description: The new do-not-disturb module (canTrigger, recordTrigger, recordIgnore, recordUserInteraction) is exported from persona-engine/index.ts but is NOT imported or used in setupPersonaEngine -- the only live entry point. Per integration rules, code that is not reachable from any entry point is dead-on-arrival. The DND frequency limits, quiet hours, and progressive backoff are fully tested in isolation but never actually guard trigger evaluation in production. This means the DND system is unreachable dead code despite having tests.
findings.suggestion: Wire do-not-disturb into setupPersonaEngine.evaluate(): call canTrigger() before evaluateTriggers(), call recordTrigger() after a trigger fires, and expose recordIgnore/recordUserInteraction in the returned API. Add an integration test proving the full flow: trigger -> DND check -> response.

findings.title: setupPersonaEngine has zero test coverage
findings.description: setupPersonaEngine is the Imperative Shell entry point that composes evaluateTriggers, createEmotionActor, generateResponse, and mapToAnimaEmotion. It manages mutable state (lastTriggerTimes, intimacy). There is no dedicated test file for this service. The persona-engine.test.ts integration test exercises the pure functions directly but does NOT test setupPersonaEngine itself. The stateful orchestration (lastTriggerTimes mutation on line 51, emotion actor lifecycle, repeated evaluate calls) is untested.
findings.suggestion: Create apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/anima/__tests__/persona-engine.test.ts that tests setupPersonaEngine directly: (1) evaluate returns null when no trigger fires, (2) evaluate returns a ProactiveResponse when trigger fires, (3) calling evaluate twice within cooldown returns null on second call (tests lastTriggerTimes statefulness), (4) dispose stops the emotion actor cleanly.

findings.title: Only 1 of 11 triggers has response templates; 10 triggers silently fallback
findings.description: RESPONSE_TEMPLATES only contains entries for 'rest-reminder' (T03). The other 10 triggers (morning-greeting, noon-care, entertainment-switch, late-night-work, evening-summary, important-date, task-due, high-frequency-switch, big-task-complete, return-to-desktop) all fall through to DEFAULT_MESSAGE. The response-generator.test.ts only tests T03 and an 'unknown-trigger' -- it does not verify that all 11 defined triggers produce meaningful user-facing messages. When T01/T02/T04-T11 fire in production, every user will see the generic fallback message regardless of emotion state.
findings.suggestion: Add response templates for all 11 triggers in RESPONSE_TEMPLATES, or at minimum add a test that iterates ALL_TRIGGERS and verifies each produces a non-fallback message. This ensures new triggers are not accidentally deployed with only generic responses. Example test: ALL_TRIGGERS.forEach(t => { const resp = generateResponse({triggered: true, triggerId: t.id, triggerName: t.name, suggestedEmotion: t.suggestedEmotion}, t.suggestedEmotion); expect(resp.message).not.toBe(DEFAULT_MESSAGE); })

findings.title: ENTERTAINMENT_APPS hardcoded as static set, not configurable
findings.code_snippet:
const ENTERTAINMENT_APPS = new Set([
  'YouTube', 'Netflix', 'Spotify', 'Bilibili',
  'Steam', 'Discord', 'Twitter', 'TikTok',
  'WeChat', 'Telegram',
])
findings.description: The entertainment app list is hardcoded as a module-level constant. It uses exact string matching against app names, which is both fragile (app names vary by OS, locale, and version -- e.g., 'Google Chrome' vs 'Chrome') and not user-configurable. Discord and WeChat are classified as 'entertainment' but are commonly used for work communication. Users have no way to customize this classification. Additionally, T04 uses exact case-sensitive Set.has() matching, so 'youtube' or 'YOUTUBE' would not match.
findings.suggestion: Accept the entertainment app list as a configuration parameter (similar to DoNotDisturbConfig). Use case-insensitive matching. Consider allowing per-user overrides. At minimum, document that app names must match exactly.

findings.title: Only rest-reminder has response templates; 10 triggers have no templates
findings.description: RESPONSE_TEMPLATES only contains entries for 'rest-reminder'. When any of the 10 new triggers fire (morning-greeting, noon-care, entertainment-switch, late-night-work, evening-summary, important-date, task-due, high-frequency-switch, big-task-complete, return-to-desktop), generateResponse will fall through to DEFAULT_MESSAGE which is a generic Chinese string. This means all 10 new triggers produce identical, context-free messages, making the trigger-specific behavior meaningless to the user.
findings.suggestion: Add at least one template per trigger name in RESPONSE_TEMPLATES. Even a single emotion variant per trigger would provide meaningful differentiation. Alternatively, accept templates as a parameter to make the response generator extensible.

findings.title: indexOf returns -1 for unknown IntimacyStage, silently wrong result
findings.code_snippet: return INTIMACY_ORDER.indexOf(current) >= INTIMACY_ORDER.indexOf(required)
findings.description: If either `current` or `required` is not present in INTIMACY_ORDER (e.g., a runtime value cast through `as IntimacyStage` or received from an external boundary), `indexOf` returns -1. Comparing -1 >= -1 yields true, meaning an unknown stage would silently pass all intimacy checks. TypeScript narrows this at compile time, but at runtime boundaries (deserialized JSON, IPC messages, plugin SDK input) the value may not match the union. This is a fail-open condition -- unknown inputs are treated as meeting any requirement rather than being rejected.

findings.title: TRIGGER_EVENT_MAP only maps rest-reminder; new triggers have no emotion events
findings.description: The TRIGGER_EVENT_MAP in the app integration service only handles 'rest-reminder'. When T01-T02 or T04-T11 fire, the event lookup at line 53 (`const event = TRIGGER_EVENT_MAP[result.triggerName]`) returns undefined, so emotionActor.send() is skipped. The emotion machine never transitions based on these new triggers. However, TriggerResult now includes suggestedEmotion which could drive the emotion transition but is not used by the integration service.
findings.suggestion: Either expand TRIGGER_EVENT_MAP to cover all 11 trigger names, or use result.suggestedEmotion to dynamically derive the emotion event instead of relying on a static map.

findings.title: No test proving canTrigger and evaluateTriggers compose correctly
findings.description: canTrigger (DND guard) and evaluateTriggers (trigger evaluation) are designed to work together: canTrigger should gate whether evaluateTriggers is called, and evaluateTriggers accepts a cooldownMultiplier from DND state. However, no test exercises this composition. The evaluateTriggers cooldownMultiplier parameter is tested in proactive-trigger.test.ts, but it's not tested in the context where the multiplier comes from recordIgnore's progressive backoff. This gap means the contract between the two modules could break silently.
findings.suggestion: Add a composition test in persona-engine.test.ts: (1) Create DND state, (2) recordIgnore 3 times to trigger backoff, (3) Verify state.cooldownMultiplier is 1.5, (4) Pass that multiplier to evaluateTriggers and verify cooldown is extended. This proves the two modules integrate correctly.

findings.title: ONE_HOUR_MS and TWENTY_FOUR_HOURS_MS duplicated across modules
findings.code_snippet:
const ONE_HOUR_MS = 60 * 60 * 1000
const THIRTY_MINUTES_MS = 30 * 60 * 1000
const TWENTY_FOUR_HOURS_MS = 24 * ONE_HOUR_MS
findings.description: The constants ONE_HOUR_MS and TWENTY_FOUR_HOURS_MS are defined identically in both proactive-trigger.ts (lines 10, 16) and do-not-disturb.ts (lines 3-4). This duplication creates a maintenance risk where one could be updated without the other.
findings.suggestion: Extract shared time constants to a common location (e.g., a constants.ts file in the persona-engine package) and import them in both modules.

findings.title: canTrigger has 6 parameters -- consider a config/context object
findings.description: The canTrigger function accepts 6 positional parameters. This exceeds the recommended threshold of 5 and forces callers to remember the exact argument order (e.g., 'currentHour' vs 'now' are both numbers, easy to swap). Grouping the context parameters into a single object would improve call-site readability and make the function easier to extend.

findings.title: ProactiveResponse fields lack readonly modifiers
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 7/10, Usefulness: 7/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 6.0). Every other interface in types.ts uses readonly modifiers (TriggerInput, TriggerCondition, IntimacyState, DoNotDisturbConfig, DoNotDisturbState, AnimaEmotionPayload, PersonaTemplate), but ProactiveResponse has plain mutable fields. This breaks the immutability convention established throughout the Functional Core. Since ProactiveResponse is returned from generateResponse() and flows through the system, it should be immutable like all other data types.

findings.title: isFrequencyExceeded silently accepts zero/negative config limits
findings.description: If config.maxPerHour or config.maxPerDay is 0 or negative (misconfigured or default-constructed with bad data), the frequency check will always report 'exceeded', silently blocking all triggers without any indication of misconfiguration. There is no input validation on the DoNotDisturbConfig values. Since this is Functional Core, a Result type or assertion would be more appropriate than silently accepting invalid configuration that produces surprising behavior.

findings.title: DoNotDisturbConfig accepts invalid hour/count values
findings.description: Encapsulation: 6/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 7/10, Enforcement: 3/10 (Aggregate: 5.0). The factory function createDefaultConfig() returns hardcoded valid values, but the DoNotDisturbConfig interface imposes no type-level or runtime constraints. A consumer can construct { maxPerHour: -1, quietHoursStart: 99, backoffMultiplier: 0 } which would cause subtle bugs (negative frequency limits, invalid hour comparisons, zero/negative backoff breaking progressive cooldown). Since DoNotDisturbConfig is a Functional Core type that controls behavior, the factory should validate invariants: hours in 0-23, counts > 0, backoffMultiplier > 1.0.

findings.title: evaluateTriggers has 5 parameters -- borderline for clarity
findings.description: evaluateTriggers sits at 5 parameters (the P2 threshold). Three of them (lastTriggerTimes, now, cooldownMultiplier) are evaluation context that travels together. Grouping these into an options object would keep the signature stable when more evaluation context is inevitably needed (e.g., DND state integration).

findings.title: TriggerInput hour/minute fields accept any number without validation
findings.code_snippet:
readonly currentHour: number
  readonly currentMinute: number
findings.description: Encapsulation: 6/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 6/10, Enforcement: 3/10 (Aggregate: 4.75). TriggerInput.currentHour should be 0-23 and currentMinute should be 0-59, but both are typed as plain number. Multiple trigger checks rely on these values being valid (T01 checks hour >= 6 && hour < 11, T02 converts to minutes for lunch time range, T05 checks for late night hours). Invalid values (e.g., hour = -1 or hour = 30) would silently produce wrong results. Since TriggerInput is the boundary type for external data entering the Functional Core, validation is critical here.

findings.title: EmotionState alias JSDoc duplicates values that will rot
findings.code_snippet:
/**
 * Type alias so response-generator and proactive-trigger can refer to
 * emotion states without importing PersonaEmotion directly.
 * Values: idle, curious, caring, worried, sleepy, excited.
 */
export type EmotionState = PersonaEmotion
findings.description: The JSDoc for EmotionState explicitly lists all six values ('idle, curious, caring, worried, sleepy, excited') as prose. These values are already visible in the PersonaEmotion type definition 4 lines above. When the PersonaEmotion union changes, this comment will become stale. The comment also explains an internal aliasing rationale ('so response-generator and proactive-trigger can refer to emotion states without importing PersonaEmotion directly') but since EmotionState IS PersonaEmotion, consumers can import either -- the alias provides no decoupling benefit worth documenting.
findings.suggestion: Remove the enumerated values from the comment. Keep only the rationale line if desired: '/** Type alias for convenience across persona-engine modules. */'

findings.title: T02_NOON_CARE boundary at exactly 13:30 not tested
findings.description: The test verifies 13:31 does not fire, but does not verify the exact boundary at 13:30 (minute 810). The check uses `<= 13 * 60 + 30`, so 13:30 should fire (inclusive upper bound). This boundary case is not explicitly tested. Similarly, T05_LATE_NIGHT does not test hour=4 (last firing hour) vs hour=5 (first non-firing hour). Boundary conditions are where off-by-one errors hide.
findings.suggestion: Add explicit boundary tests: (1) T02 at exactly 13:30 should fire, (2) T05 at hour=4 should fire, at hour=5 should not fire. Example: it('fires at boundary: 13:30', () => { expect(T02_NOON_CARE.check(makeTriggerInput({continuousWorkDurationMs: TWO_HOURS_MS + 1, currentHour: 13, currentMinute: 30}))).toBe(true) })

findings.title: TriggerInput fields lack boundary validation
findings.description: evaluateTriggers and individual trigger check functions do not validate that input fields are within expected ranges (e.g., currentHour should be 0-23, currentMinute 0-59, cooldownMultiplier > 0). While this is pure domain logic called from trusted internal code, a negative cooldownMultiplier or currentHour of -1 would produce incorrect results silently. This is low severity since TriggerInput is constructed internally.
findings.suggestion: Consider adding a debug-mode assertion or Valibot schema validation for TriggerInput at the boundary where it is constructed (in the app integration layer), not inside the pure function itself.

findings.title: Duration/timestamp fields use bare number without unit distinction
findings.code_snippet:
readonly continuousWorkDurationMs: number
  readonly previousFocusDurationMs: number
  readonly timeSinceLastActivityMs: number
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 4.75). Multiple fields represent durations in milliseconds (continuousWorkDurationMs, previousFocusDurationMs, timeSinceLastActivityMs, cooldownMs) and timestamps (triggerTimestamps, lastTriggerTimes). All are typed as plain number. The 'Ms' suffix in field names is the only protection against unit confusion. A branded type like `type Milliseconds = number & { __brand: 'ms' }` would provide compile-time safety. This is a known codebase-wide pattern (previously flagged), kept at P3 since the naming convention provides adequate runtime clarity and the walking skeleton context reduces urgency.

findings.title: ONE_HOUR_MS defined in two separate modules
findings.code_snippet:
const ONE_HOUR_MS = 60 * 60 * 1000
const TWENTY_FOUR_HOURS_MS = 24 * ONE_HOUR_MS
findings.description: ONE_HOUR_MS and TWENTY_FOUR_HOURS_MS are independently defined in both proactive-trigger.ts (lines 10, 16) and do-not-disturb.ts (lines 3-4) with identical values. While the "three similar lines > premature abstraction" guideline applies to logic, shared named constants are a straightforward extract. A single time-constants module would eliminate the duplication and ensure consistency if values ever change.
findings.suggestion:
BEFORE:
// proactive-trigger.ts
const ONE_HOUR_MS = 60 * 60 * 1000
const THIRTY_MINUTES_MS = 30 * 60 * 1000
const TWENTY_FOUR_HOURS_MS = 24 * ONE_HOUR_MS

// do-not-disturb.ts
const ONE_HOUR_MS = 60 * 60 * 1000
const TWENTY_FOUR_HOURS_MS = 24 * ONE_HOUR_MS

AFTER:
// time-constants.ts
export const ONE_HOUR_MS = 60 * 60 * 1000
export const THIRTY_MINUTES_MS = 30 * 60 * 1000
export const TWENTY_FOUR_HOURS_MS = 24 * ONE_HOUR_MS

WHY: Eliminates duplication across two modules, ensures a single source of truth for time constants within the persona-engine package.

findings.title: evaluateTriggers lastTriggerTimes uses Record<string, number>
findings.code_snippet: lastTriggerTimes: Record<string, number>,
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 4.75). The lastTriggerTimes parameter accepts any string key, but it should only contain keys matching trigger IDs (T01-T11). Using Record<string, number> allows typos and invalid keys to pass silently. A more precise type would use the trigger ID as the key type. However, since trigger conditions are extensible (the function accepts any TriggerCondition[]), a fully constrained key type would be overly rigid.
findings.suggestion:
Consider a type alias to document intent without over-constraining:

type TriggerTimesMap = Record<string, number>
// Or if triggers become a closed set:
type TriggerId = `T${string}`
type TriggerTimesMap = Partial<Record<TriggerId, number>>

findings.title: Time constant JSDoc comments restate the variable name
findings.description: Five constants (TWO_HOURS_MS, COOLDOWN_MS, ONE_HOUR_MS, THIRTY_MINUTES_MS, TWENTY_FOUR_HOURS_MS) each have a JSDoc comment that restates what the variable name and expression already convey. For example, '/** Two hours in milliseconds */' on 'TWO_HOURS_MS = 2 * 60 * 60 * 1000' adds zero information. The names are self-documenting and the arithmetic expressions are trivially readable.
findings.suggestion: Remove these comments entirely. The variable names and expressions are self-documenting. The one exception is COOLDOWN_MS which could have a comment explaining which cooldown it is for (T03 rest-reminder default), but currently it just says 'Ninety minutes cooldown in milliseconds'.

findings.title: do-not-disturb.test.ts section header '--- State Mutations ---' is inaccurate
findings.code_snippet: // --- State Mutations ---
findings.description: The section comment says 'State Mutations' but the functions tested (recordTrigger, recordIgnore, recordUserInteraction) all return new immutable state objects rather than mutating state. The DoNotDisturbState interface uses readonly fields. This comment could mislead a future maintainer into thinking these functions perform in-place mutation.
findings.suggestion: Rename to '// --- State Transitions ---' or '// --- State Updates ---' to accurately reflect the immutable return-new-state pattern.

findings.title: canTrigger could use early returns instead of if-else chain
findings.description: This is already well-structured with guard clauses. The only minor simplification is that the final frequency check assigns to a temporary variable used once. Inlining it marginally reduces cognitive load, though the current form is perfectly readable. This is a very minor observation.

findings.title: Section separator comments in proactive-trigger.ts are low-ROI
findings.description: Eleven section separator comments like '// --- T01: Morning Greeting ---' appear throughout the file. The trigger constants are already named T01_MORNING_GREETING, T02_NOON_CARE, etc., making the separators redundant. These separators also appear duplicated in the test file. While they provide minor visual structure, the maintenance cost of keeping them in sync with the constant names is not zero and the benefit is marginal in a file of this size (278 lines).
findings.suggestion: Consider removing the section separator comments. The constants are self-named and the file is short enough that IDE outline/folding provides equivalent navigation. If kept, they are harmless but provide negative ROI.

findings.title: Test comment says '23 PM' instead of '23:00'
findings.code_snippet: it('handles boundary: 23 PM is quiet', () => {
findings.description: The test description says '23 PM' which is not a valid 12-hour time format. 23:00 is in 24-hour format. Using 'PM' with 23 is contradictory. This is a minor inaccuracy in a test name that could cause momentary confusion.
findings.suggestion: Change to 'handles boundary: 23:00 is quiet' for correctness.

positive_observations: Excellent Functional Core design: all trigger conditions are pure functions with no side effects, making them trivially testable via direct instantiation with no mocks needed.
Discriminated union TriggerResult type makes illegal states unrepresentable -- good type design.
DoNotDisturb module uses immutable state pattern correctly: every mutation returns a new state object, enabling easy persistence and time-travel debugging.
Comprehensive boundary-value testing for all 11 triggers, including edge cases for time ranges (inclusive/exclusive bounds).
Clean separation of concerns: trigger evaluation, DND policy, emotion mapping, and response generation are each in their own module with clear single responsibilities.
TriggerInput uses readonly properties throughout, preventing accidental mutation.
The cooldownMultiplier parameter elegantly connects the DND backoff system to the trigger evaluation without coupling the modules directly.
All Functional Core modules tested with direct instantiation and zero mocks -- exactly the correct approach per architecture rules.
makeTriggerInput helper with Partial overrides is a clean pattern for test data construction that keeps tests readable and DRY.
persona-engine.test.ts end-to-end integration test proves real collaboration across trigger -> emotion machine -> emotion bridge -> response generator.
INTIMACY_ORDER and meetsIntimacyRequirement are thoroughly tested with stranger-through-soulmate boundary coverage.
All changed code is pure Functional Core: no I/O, no async, no try/catch needed.
Response generator uses a deliberate fallback chain (emotion -> idle -> DEFAULT_MESSAGE) with clear semantics, not silent error swallowing.
No empty catch blocks, no fire-and-forget async, no swallowed errors anywhere in the changed code.
All new interfaces use readonly modifiers consistently, establishing strong immutability conventions for Functional Core types.
String literal unions (PersonaEmotion, IntimacyStage, TriggerPriority, AppCategory) provide excellent type safety.
TriggerCondition embeds a pure check function, cleanly separating trigger logic from evaluation infrastructure.
TriggerInput is intentionally decoupled from context-engine's ActivityContext, maintaining clean module boundaries.
JSDoc on all exported interfaces and public functions in types.ts is thorough and accurate.
All functions are short (under 30 lines), with consistently low cyclomatic complexity.
Named constants are used consistently instead of magic numbers for all time durations.
The NOT_TRIGGERED constant avoids repeated object allocation and communicates intent.
Test helper functions (makeTriggerInput, stateWith) effectively reduce test boilerplate with clean partial-override patterns.
